unit.name: Using RNNs and LSTMs for NLP tasks
unit.description: Covers common RNN/LSTM use cases, conditional language models, bidirectional encoders, and stacked recurrent layers.
unit.summary: The lecture broadens from language modeling to general sequence tasks, showing that LSTMs can tag parts of speech or named entities, encode full sentences for sentiment classification, and support generation tasks like speech recognition, summarization, and translation as conditional language models [ts=2923s] [ts=3046s]. For sentence classification, it contrasts using the final hidden state with pooling over all hidden states by mean or elementwise max [ts=2979s] [ts=3004s]. It then introduces bidirectional LSTMs, where forward and backward recurrent states are concatenated so each token representation includes both left and right context, while noting such models are for analysis/encoding rather than generation [ts=3115s] [ts=3163s]. Finally, it presents stacked RNNs as a way to add vertical depth, observing that two or three LSTM layers were commonly useful in practice [ts=3214s] [ts=3251s].

key_points.text: LSTMs can be used for sequence labeling tasks such as part-of-speech tagging and named entity recognition.
For sentiment classification, a sentence can be encoded by the final hidden state or by pooling over all hidden states.
Conditional language models generate text conditioned on another input source, such as speech or a source sentence.
A bidirectional LSTM concatenates forward and backward states so each token representation sees both left and right context.
Stacked RNNs add multiple recurrent layers, with two or three layers often giving practical gains.

unit_summary_for_generation: The lecture broadens from language modeling to general sequence tasks, showing that LSTMs can tag parts of speech or named entities, encode full sentences for sentiment classification, and support generation tasks like speech recognition, summarization, and translation as conditional language models [ts=2923s] [ts=3046s]. For sentence classification, it contrasts using the final hidden state with pooling over all hidden states by mean or elementwise max [ts=2979s] [ts=3004s]. It then introduces bidirectional LSTMs, where forward and backward recurrent states are concatenated so each token representation includes both left and right context, while noting such models are for analysis/encoding rather than generation [ts=3115s] [ts=3163s]. Finally, it presents stacked RNNs as a way to add vertical depth, observing that two or three LSTM layers were commonly useful in practice [ts=3214s] [ts=3251s].

kp_catalog.name: RNN/LSTM applications and conditional language models
kp_catalog.description: LSTMs can label sequences, encode whole sentences, and generate outputs conditioned on other inputs.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because RNN/LSTM applications and conditional language models captures the core idea that LSTMs can label sequences, encode whole sentences, and generate outputs conditioned on other inputs.

kp_catalog.name: Bidirectional and stacked recurrent networks
kp_catalog.description: Forward-backward encoders and multi-layer recurrent stacks improve contextual representations and model capacity.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because Bidirectional and stacked recurrent networks captures the core idea that Forward-backward encoders and multi-layer recurrent stacks improve contextual representations and model capacity.
